Farn Polrrrcal PRACTTcEs CouurssroN
428 J Street r Suite 620 r Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 r Fax (916) 322-0886
September 5,2007
Robert E. Murphy
City Attorney
City of West Sacramento
400 Capitol Mall, 276 Floor
Sacramento, CA95814
Re:
Your Request for Advice
Our FiIe No. .4,-07-134
I The Political Reform Act is contained in Government code Sections g1000 through 91014. All
statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulationf of the Fair
PoliticalPracticescommissionarecontainedinsectionsl8ll0throughlsggTofTitle 
2ofthecalifornia
Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division'6 of the California Code of
Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.
Dear Mr. Murphy:
This letter is in response to your request for advice on behalf of West Sacramento
Councilmember Mark Johannessen regarding the conflict-of-interest provisions of the
Political Reform Act (the'Act').r Nothing in ttis letter should be construed to evaluate
any conduct that may have already taken place, and any conclusions contained in this
letter apply only to prospective actions. In addition, this letter is based on the facts
presented. The Fair Political Practices Commission ("the Commission') does not act as a
finder of fact when it renders assistance. (In re oglesby (lg7 5) I Fppc ops. 71 .)
Also, please note that our advice is based solely on the provisions of the Act. We
therefore offer no opinion on the application, if any, oiother conflict-of-interest laws
such as colnmon law conflict of interest or Govemment code Section 1090.
QUESTIONS
1. May Councilmember Johannessen make, participate in making, or influence
governmental decisions to finance engineering studies or to hire consultiig firms to
review facility and environmental concerns for the approximately 45 mileJof levees
protecting the city of west sacramento (the "city") despite o*rirg a property within
500 feet of one of the nine portions of the levee system?-


File No. A-07-134
Page No. 2
2. May Councilmember Johannessen make, parlicipate in making, or influence
governmental decisions related to the engineering solutions or facilities identified as
necessaryupon the completion of the city-wide engineering evaluation of the flood
protection infr astructure?
CONCLUSIONS
1. Baring additional facts, Councilmember Johannessen maymake, participate
in making, or influence the governmental decisions to finance engineering studies or to
hire consulting firms to review facility and environrnental concerns for the 45 miles of
levees protecting the City.
2. Without the factual context of a particular decision, we are unable to reach a
conclusion regarding the participation of Councilmember Johannessen in any decisions
related to engineering solutions or facilities identified by the city-wide engineering
evaluation of the flood protection infrastructure.
FACTS
Mark Johannessen is a member of the West Sacramento City Council (the..City
Council"). Councilmember Johannessen and his wife own an approximately four-acre
property within the City. This four-acre property is valued at more than $2,b00.
Beginning in2006, the City and the West Sacramento Area Flood Control
Agency, in cooperation with the State of Califomia and various federal agencies have
undertaken a city-wide engineering evaluation of flood protection infrastircfure. General
policy discussion will address, among other things, the means of financing engineer
studies, which consulting firms to engage for facility and environmental rivieiv, and
engineering solutions and facilities for each of the various portions of the rivers and
canals surrounding the City. The evaluation of the flood piotection infrastructure is to be
divided into nine portions of the approximatery 45 miles of l"rr""r. A subsequent
engineer's report and study will identify necessary repairs or improvements of levees for
each of the nine portions.
Councilmember Johannessen's four-acre property is located within 500 feet of
one of the nine portions of levees that will be evaluated.
ANALYSIS
Section 871 0O prohibits any public official from makin g, participating in making,
or otherwise using his or her official position to influence a govemmental decision in
which the official has a financial interest. A public official has a o'financial interes t, in a
governmental decision, within the meaning of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that
the governmental decision will have a material financial effect on one or more of the
public official's economic interests. (section g7103; Regulation 1g700(a).) The


File No. A-07-134
Page No. 3
Commission has adopted an eight-step standard analysis for deciding whether an
individual has a disquali$ring conflict of interest in a given govemmental decision, which
we apply to your question. (Regulation 18700(bX1)_(g).)
step one: Is councilmember Johannessen a ,.public official?r,
The Act's conflict of interest provisions apply only to "public officials.,,
(Sections 87100, 87103:' Regulation r s700(bx1l.) e ,puLfic o^fficial,, is ..every member,
officer, employee or consultant of a state or local government agency ....,,
(Section 82048.) As a member of the City Council, Councilm.*b"iloh*oessen is a
public official within the meaning of the Act.2
step Two: rs councilmember Johannessen making, participating in making, or
influencing a governmental decision?
A public officia-l"makes a govemmental decision" when the official, acting
within the authority of his or her office or position, votes on a matter, obligates or
commits his or her agency to any course of action, or enters into any co nt.r-acrual
agreement on behalf of his or her agency. (Regulati on 18702.L) A public official
"participates in a govemmental decision" when, acting within the auihority oitris or her
position and without significant intervening substantiie review, the official negotiates,
advises, or makes recommendations to the decisionmaker regarding the governmental
decision. (Regulation 1s702.2.) A public official is attempting to ise hi-s or her official
position to influence a decision if for the purpose of influencing, the official contacts or
appears before any member, officer, employee, or consultant of his or her agency.
(Regulation 18702.3.) Councilmember Johannessen is making, participatiigin making,
or influencing a governmental decision when acting in his rolJas aciiycoincil member.
Step Three: What are Councilmember Johannessenrs ,6economic interests?r,
A public official has a financial interest in a decision within the meaning of
Section 87103 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material
financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the official, a
member of his or her immediate family, or on any one of five enumerated economic
interests, including:
o An economic interest in a business entity in which he or she has a direct or indirect
investment of $2,000 or more (Section 87103(a); Regulation 1g703.1(a); or in which
2 If a public official's office is listed in Section 87200 (*87200 filers,, include members of a city
council) and he or she has a conflict of interest in a decision noticed at a public meeting, then he or she
must (l)immediatelyprior to the discussion of the item, verbally identifr each type oil.ooo-i" interest
involved in the decision as well as details ofthe economic interest, as dislussed io n"grtutior, -
18702'5(bXl)(B), on the record of the meeting; (2) recuse himselfor herself; and (3) i"eave the room for the
duration ofthe discussion and/or vote on the iiem. For closed sessions, consent calendars, absences and
speaking as a member of the public regarding personal interests, special rules found in n"gutution 1g702.5,
subdivisions (c) and (d) apply. (Section 87105.)


File No. A-07-134
Page No. 4
he or she is a director, officer, parlner,trustee, employee, or holds any position of
management (Section 87 1 03(d); Regulation 1 g703. I (b));
o An economic interest in real property in which he or she has a direct or indirect
interest of $2,000 or more (Section s7103(b); Regulation lg7o3.2);
o An economic interest in any source of income, including promised income,
aggtegating $500 or more within 12 months prior to the decision (Section g7103(c);
Regulation 18703.3);
o An economic interest in any source of gifts to him or her if the gifts aggregateto $390
or more within 12 months prior to the decision (Section 87103(e); Relilation
18703.4);
o An economic interest in his or her personal finances, including those of his or her
immediate family -- this is the "personal financial effects" rule (Sectiong7l03;
Regulation 18703.5).
From the facts submitted the only economic interest indicated is Councilmember
Johannessen's economic interest in real property. Because you have not provided any
facts indicating that any other economic interest may be affected, our anaiysis is limited
to councilmember Johannessen's economic interest in his real property.3 
-
Step Four: Is Councilmember Johannessen's economic interest direcdy or indirectly
involved in the decision?
Real Property:
Regulation 18704.2(a) states that realproperty in which a public official has an
economic interest is directly involved in a governmental decisiorrif uny of the following
apply:
"(1) The real property in which the official has an interest, or any
part of that real property, is located within 500 feet of the boundaries (oi
the proposedboundaries) of the property which is the subject of the
governmental decision. . ..
"Q)The governmental decision involves the zoning or rczoning
annexation or deannexation, sale, purchase, or lease, or inclusion in or
exclusion from any city, county, district or other local governmental
A public official always has an economic interest in his or her personal finances. However, any
financial effect a decision may have on Councilmember Johannessen's real properry is considered an effect
on his real property interests and would notbe analyzed separately under the "personal financial effects,,
rules' (Regulation 18705.5(a).) Accordingly, the p".ronuffirrarcial effects rule does not appear to apply to
councilmember Johannessen's circumstances and we will not discuss it further.


File No. A-07-134
Page No. 5
subdivision, of the real property in which the official has an interest or a
similar decision affecting the real property ....
"(3) The govefirmental decision involves the issuance, denial or
revocation of a license, permit or other land use entitlemen t authoizing a
specific use or uses of the real property in which the official has an
interest.
"(4) The governmental decision involves the imposition, repeal, or
modification of any taxes or fees assessed or imposed on the real property
in which the official has an interest.
"(5) The goveflrmental decision is to designate the survey area, to
select the project area,to adopt the prelimin*y plun,to form aproject area
committee, to certify the environmental docum*t, to adopt the
redevelopment plan, to add territory to the redevelopment area, or to
rescind or amend any of the above decisions; and real propertyin which
the official has an interest, or any part of it is located within the
boundaries (or the proposed boundaries) of the redevelopment area.
"(6) The decision involves construction of, or improvements to,
streets, water, sewer, storm drainage or similar facilitiesf and the real
property in which the official has an interest will receive new or improved
services."
Notwithstanding Regulation 1870 4.2(a),real property in which a public official
has an interest is not directly involved in a govemmentai decision, but indirectly
involved, if the "decision solely concerns repairs, replacement, or maintenance of
existing streets, water, sewer, storm drainage or similar facilities.,, (Regulation
18704.2(b)Q).)
From the facts you have provided, Councilmernber Johannessen's interest in the
four-acre property is a real property interest located within 500 feet of one of the nine
portions of levees subject to general decisions relating to the flood impact policy of the
City. Because Councilmember Johannessen'r prop"ri'y is within 500 ieet of tfr" property
subject to the governmental decision, Councilmemberlohannessen,s property is directly
involved in the decisions unless the exception of Regulation 15704.2(b)1i; appties.
Flood control work presents a unique question of interpretation. By design, flood
control work is performed to increase protection from flooding. Howev"rjth" majority
of flood control work for a city protected by a system of levees could just as easily be
classified as "repairs" or "maintenance" as opposed to an o,improvement,,, despite the fact
that the work will in some way increase the "ity'r flood prote.tio.r. (See CauileAdvice
Letter, l-06-179.)


File No. A-07-134
Page No. 6
Under the facts provided, the City is currently in the process of evaluatin gthe 45
miles of levees protecting the city and will soon be uaar"s.irrg the financing of engineer
studies, the hiring of consulting firms to review both facility ind.ruiron-.lrtal concerns,
and the engineering solutions and facilities necessary for each portion of the levees. The
reports from these firms will identifr necessary repairs or improvements for the levees.
Addressing your questions related to the City Council,s decisions to finance
engineering studies or to hire consulting firms to.",ni.lr facility and environmental
concerns for the approximately 45 miles of levees protecting the City, *. Lor"rrrae that
these decisions are de.cisions solely concerning the "repair" or.,maintenance,, of the levee
system' Thus, Councilmember Johannessen's real property interest is only indirectly
involved in these decisions.
In terms of decisions related to engineering solutions or facilities identified by the
evaluation of the system, each solution oifacilityinust be evaluated independently to
determine whethel it is a 'Tepaif' or the ,,maintenance,, of the levee.yrt.ii o. -
"improvement'" Generally, aspecific engineering solution or facility will be considered
a"repair" or the "maintenance" of the levee systein if the solution or facility is necessary
to provide the level ofprotection deemed reasonable for the protection of the entire city.
However, a proposed solution or facility providing protection for an official's property
interest beyond the level of protection provided fo-r ttre remainder of the city -u.t u"
considered an "improvement.,,
Because y9u hav-e not limited y.our questions to a particular decision relating to
proposed engineering solutions or facilities, *" ur" unable to determine whether
councilmember Johannessen's real property interest is directly or indirectif involved in
any particular decisions other than the dicisions to finance engineering stuaies or to hire
consulting firms to review facility and environmental concerns for the entire 45 miles of
levees.
steps Five and six: will there be a reasonahly foreseeable material financial effect
on Councilmember Johannessen rs economic interest(s)?
Materiality
. 
Having identified the economic interests involved, and determined whether each
interest is directly or indirectly involved in the decision ut i.rrr", it is necessary to identify
the materiality standard appropriate to each economic interest.
The financial effect of a governmental decision on real property directly involved
in the govemmental decision is presumed to be material. (Regulation 1g705.2(a)(1).)
This presumption may be rebutted only by proof that it is not reasonably foreseeable that
the govemmental decision will have any financial effect on the properti o, inlirr.,
words not even a single penny,s effect on the property.


File No. A-07-t34
Page No. 7
- 
For property indirectly involved in a governmental decision, any financial effect
of the decision on_the property is presumed not to be materiar. (Reguliion
18705'2(b)(1).) However, this presumption may be rebutted uy'pro-ortnat there are
specific circumstances regarding the govefirmental decision, its financial effect, and the
nature of the real property in which the public official has an economic interest that make
it reasonably foreseeable the decision will have amaterial financialeffect on the real
property in which the official has an interest. Examples of these specinc ciicumstances
include circumstances where the decision affects:
"(A) The development potential or income producing potential of
. 
the real property in which the official has an economic interEst;
"(B) The use of the real property in which the official has an
economic interest;
"(C) The character of the neighborhood including, but not limited
to, substantial effects on: traffic, view, pivacy,intensity of use, noise
levels, air emissions, or similar traits of the neighborhood.,,
Foreseeability
once a public official has determined the materiality standards applicable to each
of his or her economic interests, the next step is determining whether it iis'..reasonably
foreseeable" thatthe standard will be met. A material financial effect on an economic
interest is "reasonably foreseeable" if it is substantially likely that one or more of the
materiality standards will be met as a result of the golrl*-"rrtal decision. (Regulation
18706(a)') An effect need not be certain to be conJidered "reasonably foreseeable,,, but it
must be more than a mere possibility. (In re Thorner (1g75) I Fppcbps. rla.;
Ultimately, whether a material financial effect is foreseeable at the time a decision
is made depends on facts and circumstances peculiar to each case. (In ri riorru, supra,
at 198') Because the commission does not act as a finder of fact in proviJing advice (In
re oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC ops. 71), the foreseeability of a particular financial effect is a
determination that must be left, in most instances, to ih" irfo.-"d judgment of the public
official.
Turning to your questions related to the city council,s decisions to finance
engineering sfudies or to hire consulting firms to review facility and environmental
concerns' Councilmember Johannessen's interest in property is only inAire"tlvlnvolved
in these decisions. And because these decisions relie to all of the 45 miles of levees
protecting the City, there does not appear to be any indication of specific circumstances
regarding the governmental decision.its financial effect, and the nature of
councilmember Johannessen's property that make it reasonably foreseeable the decision
will have a material financial effect o, ih. property. Accordingly, uuoirg-ujaliiorrur
facts, Councilmember Johannessen -uy -ut", pu.ti"iput. in making, or influence the


File No. A-07-134
Page No. 8
governmental decisions to finance engineering studies or to hire consulting firms to
review facility and environmental concems roi ttre 45 miles orr"""". froiE",,ng the city.
However, without the facfual context of a particular decision, we are unable to
reach a conclusion regarding the participation of iouncilmember Joirannessen in any
decisions related to engineering solutions or facilities identified by the "lruirutio, of the
levee system. To assist in analyzing Councilmember Johannessen,s participation in
future decisions we have enclosed a "opy of the commission's fact .ir""t "rtitt ed,,,Carrr
vote? overview of the conflict of Interest Laws." should councilmember Johannessen
need additional assistance in determining his obligations under the Act,s conflict-of-
interests provisions as they pertain to any specific decisions, it is advisable that
Councilmember Johannessen seek further advice providing all relevant facts once the
facts become available.
Steps Seven and Eight: Does this governmental decision come within any exception
to the conflict-of-interest rules?
Even if an official has a conflict ofinterest, disqualification is not required if the
govemmental decision affects the public official's economic interests in a manner that is
indistinguishable from the manneiin which the decisio" *itt affect the public generally.
(Section 87 103 ; Regulation lg7 07 (a).)
Additionally, in certain rare circumstances, a public official may be called upon to
take part in a decision underthe "legally required parlicipation,, exceptio" alrpit" the fact
that the official may have a disqualifrirg 
"orrfli"t ormterest. This exception applies only in
certain very specific circumstances where the government agency would be paralyzed from
acting. (Section 87101; Regulation 1S70g.)
You have not presented any facts indicating that the "public generally', or the
"legally required participation" exteptions are applicable to councilmember
Johannessen's circumstances, so we will not address them further.
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (g16) 322-5660.
Sincerely,
Scott Hallabrin
General Counsel
By: Brian G. Lau
Counsel, Legal Division
BGLjgl
Enclosure

In terms of decisions related to engineering solutions or facilities identified by the
evaluation of the system, each solution oifacilityinust be evaluated independently to

"improvement'" Generally, aspecific engineering solution or facility will be considered
a"repair" or the "maintenance" of the levee systein if the solution or facility is necessary
to provide the level ofprotection deemed reasonable for the protection of the entire city.
However, a proposed solution or facility providing protection for an official's property
interest beyond the level of protection provided fo-r ttre remainder of the city -u.t u"

Because y9u hav-e not limited y.our questions to a particular decision relating to

steps Five and six: will there be a reasonahly foreseeable material financial effect
on Councilmember Johannessen rs economic interest(s)?
Materiality
. 
Having identified the economic interests involved, and determined whether each
interest is directly or indirectly involved in the decision ut i.rrr", it is necessary to identify
the materiality standard appropriate to each economic interest.
The financial effect of a governmental decision on real property directly involved
in the govemmental decision is presumed to be material. (Regulation 1g705.2(a)(1).)
This presumption may be rebutted only by proof that it is not reasonably foreseeable that
the govemmental decision will have any financial effect on the properti o, inlirr.,
words not even a single penny,s effect on the property.


File No. A-07-t34
- 
For property indirectly involved in a governmental decision, any financial effect
of the decision on_the property is presumed not to be materiar. (Reguliion
18705'2(b)(1).) However, this presumption may be rebutted uy'pro-ortnat there are
specific circumstances regarding the govefirmental decision, its financial effect, and the
nature of the real property in which the public official has an economic interest that make
it reasonably foreseeable the decision will have amaterial financialeffect on the real
property in which the official has an interest. Examples of these specinc ciicumstances
include circumstances where the decision affects:
"(A) The development potential or income producing potential of
. 
the real property in which the official has an economic interEst;
"(B) The use of the real property in which the official has an
economic interest;
"(C) The character of the neighborhood including, but not limited
to, substantial effects on: traffic, view, pivacy,intensity of use, noise
levels, air emissions, or similar traits of the neighborhood.,,
Foreseeability
once a public official has determined the materiality standards applicable to each
of his or her economic interests, the next step is determining whether it iis'..reasonably
foreseeable" thatthe standard will be met. A material financial effect on an economic
interest is "reasonably foreseeable" if it is substantially likely that one or more of the
materiality standards will be met as a result of the golrl*-"rrtal decision. (Regulation
18706(a)') An effect need not be certain to be conJidered "reasonably foreseeable,,, but it
must be more than a mere possibility. (In re Thorner (1g75) I Fppcbps. rla.;
Ultimately, whether a material financial effect is foreseeable at the time a decision
is made depends on facts and circumstances peculiar to each case. (In ri riorru, supra,
at 198') Because the commission does not act as a finder of fact in proviJing advice (In
re oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC ops. 71), the foreseeability of a particular financial effect is a
determination that must be left, in most instances, to ih" irfo.-"d judgment of the public
official.
Turning to your questions related to the city council,s decisions to finance
engineering sfudies or to hire consulting firms to review facility and environmental

in these decisions. And because these decisions relie to all of the 45 miles of levees

However, without the facfual context of a particular decision, we are unable to

vote? overview of the conflict of Interest Laws." should councilmember Johannessen
need additional assistance in determining his obligations under the Act,s conflict-of-
interests provisions as they pertain to any specific decisions, it is advisable that
Councilmember Johannessen seek further advice providing all relevant facts once the
facts become available.
Steps Seven and Eight: Does this governmental decision come within any exception
to the conflict-of-interest rules?
Even if an official has a conflict ofinterest, disqualification is not required if the
govemmental decision affects the public official's economic interests in a manner that is
indistinguishable from the manneiin which the decisio" *itt affect the public generally.
(Section 87 103 ; Regulation lg7 07 (a).)
Additionally, in certain rare circumstances, a public official may be called upon to
take part in a decision underthe "legally required parlicipation,, exceptio" alrpit" the fact
that the official may have a disqualifrirg 
"orrfli"t ormterest. This exception applies only in
certain very specific circumstances where the government agency would be paralyzed from
acting. (Section 87101; Regulation 1S70g.)
You have not presented any facts indicating that the "public generally', or the
"legally required participation" exteptions are applicable to councilmember
Johannessen's circumstances, so we will not address them further.

QUESTION: 1. May Councilmember Johannessen make, participate in making, or influence
governmental decisions to finance engineering studies or to hire consultiig firms to
review facility and environmental concerns for the approximately 45 mileJof levees
protecting the city of west sacramento (the "city") despite o*rirg a property within
500 feet of one of the nine portions of the levee system?-
2. May Councilmember Johannessen make, parlicipate in making, or influence
governmental decisions related to the engineering solutions or facilities identified as
necessaryupon the completion of the city-wide engineering evaluation of the flood
protection infr astructure?

CONCLUSION: 1. Baring additional facts, Councilmember Johannessen maymake, participate
in making, or influence the governmental decisions to finance engineering studies or to
hire consulting firms to review facility and environrnental concerns for the 45 miles of
levees protecting the City.
2. Without the factual context of a particular decision, we are unable to reach a
conclusion regarding the participation of Councilmember Johannessen in any decisions
related to engineering solutions or facilities identified by the city-wide engineering
evaluation of the flood protection infrastructure.

Dear Mr. Murphy: This letter is in response to your request for advice on behalf of West Sacramento Councilmember Mark Johannessen regarding the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the'Act').r Nothing in ttis letter should be construed to evaluate any conduct that may have already taken place, and any conclusions contained in this letter apply only to prospective actions. In addition, this letter is based on the facts presented. The Fair Political Practices Commission ("the Commission') does not act as a finder of fact when it renders assistance. (In re oglesby (lg7 5) I Fppc ops. 71 .) Also, please note that our advice is based solely on the provisions of the Act. We therefore offer no opinion on the application, if any, oiother conflict-of-interest laws such as colnmon law conflict of interest or Govemment code Section 1090. QUESTIONS 1. May Councilmember Johannessen make, participate in making, or influence governmental decisions to finance engineering studies or to hire consultiig firms to review facility and environmental concerns for the approximately 45 mileJof levees protecting the city of west sacramento (the "city") despite o*rirg a property within 500 feet of one of the nine portions of the levee system?- File No. A-07-134 Page No. 2 2. May Councilmember Johannessen make, parlicipate in making, or influence governmental decisions related to the engineering solutions or facilities identified as necessaryupon the completion of the city-wide engineering evaluation of the flood protection infr astructure? CONCLUSIONS 1. Baring additional facts, Councilmember Johannessen maymake, participate in making, or influence the governmental decisions to finance engineering studies or to hire consulting firms to review facility and environrnental concerns for the 45 miles of levees protecting the City. 2. Without the factual context of a particular decision, we are unable to reach a conclusion regarding the participation of Councilmember Johannessen in any decisions related to engineering solutions or facilities identified by the city-wide engineering evaluation of the flood protection infrastructure. FACTS Mark Johannessen is a member of the West Sacramento City Council (the..City Council"). Councilmember Johannessen and his wife own an approximately four-acre property within the City. This four-acre property is valued at more than $2,b00. Beginning in2006, the City and the West Sacramento Area Flood Control Agency, in cooperation with the State of Califomia and various federal agencies have undertaken a city-wide engineering evaluation of flood protection infrastircfure.
